domain: AI Agent Self-Correction & Error Recovery Systems

scoring_methodology.demand: 0-10: How many people want this? Based on validated demand signals, GitHub issues, forum posts, search volume.
scoring_methodology.competition: 0-10: How empty is this space? 10 = nobody does this, 0 = saturated market.
scoring_methodology.feasibility: 0-10: How buildable is this? 10 = solo dev in 2 weeks, 0 = requires massive infra/data.

ranked_gaps.title: Unified Agent Resilience Middleware
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A composable Python middleware layer that wraps any agent framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic AI, custom) and provides retry logic, circuit breakers, fallback providers, output validation, and crash recovery as pluggable decorators/middleware. Think 'Tenacity + pybreaker + Guardrails AI + checkpointing' in one package with a @resilient decorator.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Backend engineers and ML engineers running production AI agents across any framework. Companies with 2-50 agents in production that spend weeks stitching together resilience patterns manually. DevOps teams responsible for agent uptime SLAs.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Agent frameworks are proliferating (7+ major ones in 2026) but none provide unified resilience. Production deployments are growing 8x (Gartner: 40% of enterprise apps will have agents in 2026 vs 5% in 2025). The pain of hand-building resilience stacks is becoming acute as agents move from prototype to production.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium-High. Requires deep understanding of multiple agent frameworks' internals to build adapters. The middleware pattern itself is well-understood, but making it truly framework-agnostic requires significant integration work.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $800M-1.2B (agent infrastructure tooling). SAM: $150-300M (resilience-specific middleware). SOM: $15-30M (initial open-source + enterprise tier).

ranked_gaps.title: Multi-Agent Error Propagation Prevention
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: An inter-agent error boundary library that wraps multi-agent communication channels with output validation, quarantine zones, and cascading failure detection. Includes: (1) output validation gates between agents, (2) error blast radius containment, (3) automatic fallback to safe outputs when downstream agents detect upstream hallucinations, (4) a dashboard showing error propagation paths.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams building multi-agent workflows (customer support pipelines, research agents, code generation chains). Enterprises deploying CrewAI/AutoGen/LangGraph multi-agent systems where one bad agent can cascade failures across the entire pipeline.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Multi-agent systems are the dominant architecture pattern in 2026. Research shows 17.2x error amplification and 37% of failures are coordination breakdowns (MAST study: 1,642 traces, 41-87% failure rates). SUPERVISORAGENT paper shows the approach works (35% cost reduction, 63% variance reduction) but no productized tool exists.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: High. Requires understanding multi-agent communication patterns across frameworks, building validation gates that don't add unacceptable latency, and handling diverse output formats.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $500M-800M (multi-agent orchestration tooling). SAM: $100-200M (error prevention layer). SOM: $10-20M.

ranked_gaps.title: Intelligent Error Classification & Routing
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: An error classification middleware that intercepts agent errors, classifies them (transient/permanent, model/tool/infra, recoverable/fatal), and routes each to the optimal recovery strategy. Uses a lightweight classifier (could be rule-based + small model) to analyze error messages, stack traces, and context. Routes: transient errors to retry with backoff, model errors to prompt adjustment, tool errors to fallback tools, permanent errors to human escalation.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Any team running agents in production that wastes compute on retrying permanent errors or escalates transient errors unnecessarily. Platform teams building agent infrastructure who need to reduce MTTR and cost-per-error.
ranked_gaps.why_now: SHIELDA paper (2025) validated structured exception handling for agents. All current tools use generic retry-on-exception. As agents handle more diverse tool calls and APIs, the variety of error types is exploding. Classification could cut unnecessary retries by 60%+.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Error classification can start rule-based (HTTP status codes, known error patterns) and graduate to ML. The routing logic is straightforward. Integration as middleware is well-understood.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $400-600M (agent observability + recovery). SAM: $80-150M (error intelligence layer). SOM: $8-15M.

ranked_gaps.title: Framework-Agnostic Error Recovery SDK
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A Python SDK with @recoverable, @checkpoint, @validate decorators that work across LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic AI, Google ADK, and custom agent loops. Provides: retry with classification, output validation, state checkpointing, and human escalation -- all via simple decorators that wrap any function. Framework adapters handle the translation layer.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams using multiple agent frameworks or migrating between them. Startups and mid-size companies that don't want vendor lock-in. Developers building custom agents who want production-grade recovery without adopting a full framework.
ranked_gaps.why_now: The agent framework market is fragmenting (7+ major frameworks). GitHub issues across n8n, Google ADK, and Pydantic AI all independently request the same recovery features. No one wants to rebuild recovery logic every time they switch frameworks.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Core SDK is straightforward (decorators + middleware pattern). The challenge is building and maintaining adapters for each framework as they evolve rapidly.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $600M-1B (agent developer tools). SAM: $120-250M (cross-framework SDK). SOM: $12-25M.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Circuit Breaker Library
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A Python library that implements the circuit breaker pattern specifically for AI agent tool calls. Unlike generic circuit breakers (pybreaker), this understands agent semantics: tracks per-tool failure rates, automatically disables failing tools and notifies the agent to use alternatives, provides graceful degradation to text-only mode, and integrates with agent frameworks' tool registries.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams running agents with multiple external tool integrations (APIs, databases, search). Production agents that currently keep hammering failing tools until timeout, wasting API credits and causing cascading slowdowns.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Generic Python circuit breakers exist (pybreaker, circuitbreaker) but none understand agent tool semantics. As agents use 10-50+ tools in production, the need for intelligent per-tool circuit breaking is growing. GitHub issues explicitly request this feature.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Low-Medium. The circuit breaker pattern is well-documented. The agent-specific layer (tool registry integration, graceful degradation) adds moderate complexity. Could ship an MVP in 2-3 weeks.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $200-400M (agent reliability tooling). SAM: $40-80M (circuit breaker specific). SOM: $4-8M. Best as open-source with enterprise features.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Workflow Checkpoint & Resume for Non-LangGraph Frameworks
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A lightweight checkpoint/resume library that adds state persistence to any agent framework. Serialize agent state (conversation history, tool results, intermediate outputs) at configurable checkpoints. On failure, resume from the last checkpoint. Support multiple backends (SQLite, Redis, S3). Provide a simple API: checkpoint(state), resume(task_id).
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams running long agent workflows (10+ steps) on CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic AI, or custom frameworks who lose all progress when a step fails. Especially painful for workflows costing $5-50+ per run.
ranked_gaps.why_now: LangGraph has checkpointing and Microsoft Agent Framework added it in Feb 2026. But CrewAI, Pydantic AI, Smolagents, and Google ADK users still lack it. The gap is narrowing but still wide for most frameworks. GitHub feature requests are explicit and recent.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. State serialization is tricky (agent state can include non-serializable objects, open connections, etc.). Each framework has different state management patterns. Requires deep integration work per framework.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Error Recovery Benchmarking & Chaos Testing
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A chaos engineering toolkit for AI agents. Provides fault injectors (LLM timeout, tool failure, rate limit, hallucination injection, data corruption) and a measurement framework (recovery success rate, time-to-recover, cost-per-recovery, error amplification factor). Run chaos tests in CI/CD to ensure agent reliability before deployment.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: QA teams and SRE teams responsible for agent reliability. Enterprise teams that need to prove agent robustness before production deployment. Agent framework developers who want to benchmark recovery capabilities.
ranked_gaps.why_now: agent-chaos OSS project exists but is early stage. ReliabilityBench and ChaosEater are academic only. Uber built internal chaos testing for mobile agents (180K+ tests). The concept is validated but no turnkey tool exists for general-purpose AI agents.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Fault injection is straightforward (mock/intercept LLM calls, tool calls). The measurement framework requires some design. Integration with CI/CD is well-understood.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $300-500M (agent testing/QA). SAM: $60-120M (chaos testing specific). SOM: $6-12M.

ranked_gaps.title: Self-Healing Agent Runtime (Production-Ready)
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A runtime daemon that monitors agent behavioral patterns in real-time, diagnoses recurring failure modes, and automatically applies corrective prompt/config changes. Watches for: repeated output patterns (loops), degrading output quality, increasing error rates, anomalous tool usage. Applies fixes: prompt adjustments, temperature changes, model swaps, tool disabling.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams running always-on agents (customer support, monitoring, data pipelines) that need autonomous recovery without human intervention. Especially valuable for off-hours when no engineer is on-call.
ranked_gaps.why_now: VIGIL (2025) proved the concept academically. Multiple HN Show HN posts show developer attempts. Salesforce Agentforce 3.0 has self-healing for CRM-specific agents. But no generic, framework-agnostic self-healing runtime exists. The '70+ production bugs' DEV.to post shows real demand.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: High. Autonomous diagnosis and prompt adjustment is hard to get right without introducing new failure modes. The '4-tier self-healing agent that was silently broken for weeks' HN post illustrates the difficulty. Requires sophisticated monitoring and careful correction logic.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $1-2B (agent reliability + AIOps). SAM: $200-400M (self-healing runtime). SOM: $20-40M. High ceiling but hard to execute.

ranked_gaps.title: Real-Time Reflection & Self-Critique Layer
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A plug-and-play middleware that intercepts agent output, runs a configurable critique/verification step (using a smaller/cheaper model), and triggers self-correction before the output reaches the user or next agent. Supports custom critique prompts, confidence thresholds, and automatic retry on low-quality output.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams building customer-facing agents where output quality directly impacts user trust. Content generation pipelines, code generation agents, and research agents where hallucination is costly.
ranked_gaps.why_now: HuggingFace identified reflective agents as a 2026 trend. DSPy handles compile-time optimization but not runtime reflection. Guardrails AI validates format but not quality. The gap is specifically in real-time quality-aware self-correction.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. The middleware pattern is simple. The critique model call adds latency and cost. Configuring when to trigger reflection (every output vs. confidence-based) requires careful design.

ranked_gaps.title: Cost-Aware Retry & Recovery Budgeting
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A cost-tracking middleware that wraps agent LLM calls and tool calls, enforces per-task and per-session cost budgets for error recovery, automatically switches to cheaper models for retries, and alerts when recovery costs exceed thresholds. Dashboard shows cost-per-error, cost-per-recovery, and budget utilization.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams spending $500-10K+/month on agent API costs with no visibility into how much is spent on retries vs. productive work. Finance-conscious startups and enterprises with strict cost controls.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Google's Budget Tracker and BATS framework validate the concept. LangSmith/Helicone track costs but don't enforce budgets or manage retry-specific spending. Power users burning $30-800/month with minimal visibility (Composio report). The gap is enforcement, not tracking.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Cost tracking requires intercepting API calls (proxy pattern). Budget enforcement is straightforward. Model downgrade for retries requires model routing logic. Dashboard is standard.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $600M-1B (LLM cost management). SAM: $120-250M (retry-specific budgeting). SOM: $12-25M.

ranked_gaps.title: Agent Code Self-Repair Beyond Simple Retry
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A code repair system for coding agents that goes beyond 'feed error back to LLM'. Maintains a knowledge base of past errors and fixes, analyzes stack traces to identify root cause patterns, applies known fixes before trying LLM-based repair, detects and breaks infinite repair loops, and learns from successful repairs to prevent repeat failures.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams building or using coding agents (Cursor, Replit Agent, Claude Code, Copilot Workspace). Non-developers using AI coding tools who get stuck when agents enter error loops.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Slagent (HN) shows demand for learning from past coding errors. Coding agents entering infinite loops is a widely reported problem. The knowledge base approach (remember past fixes) is well-suited to current LLM capabilities.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium-High. The knowledge base requires embedding and retrieval. Root cause analysis from stack traces is non-trivial. Loop detection needs careful heuristics. Integration with diverse coding agents adds complexity.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $1-2B (AI coding tools). SAM: $200-400M (code repair/reliability). SOM: $20-40M.

ranked_gaps.title: Rollback & Undo for Agent Side Effects
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A side-effect tracking and rollback system for agents that take real-world actions. Logs all agent actions with enough context to generate compensating transactions. Provides undo capabilities for reversible actions and human-approval gates for irreversible ones. Integrates with common APIs to provide automated rollback (e.g., unsend email, revert database change, cancel API call).
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Enterprise teams deploying agents that modify production systems (CRM updates, database writes, email sending, API calls). Regulated industries where agent mistakes have compliance implications.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Rubrik launched Agent Rewind (Aug 2025) for data-layer rollback. Cohesity is building a competitor. But these are enterprise-grade, data-focused tools. No developer-friendly SDK exists for general-purpose agent action rollback at the application layer.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Very High. Every API and action type needs a custom compensating transaction. Many actions are inherently irreversible. The scope is enormous. Best approached as a narrow-scope MVP (e.g., database rollback only, then expand).
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $1-2B (agent governance/safety). SAM: $200-400M (rollback tooling). SOM: $5-10M initially. Rubrik/Cohesity targeting enterprise; dev-tool opportunity at lower end.

ranked_gaps.title: Long-Running Agent Heartbeat & Watchdog
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A watchdog service for long-running agents that monitors heartbeats, detects stuck agents (infinite loops, hangs, silent failures), kills stuck processes, and either retries or escalates. Provides dead-man's-switch functionality. Configurable thresholds for different agent types.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams running agents on long tasks (hours/days): data processing, research, monitoring. DevOps teams responsible for agent uptime who need to detect silent failures.
ranked_gaps.why_now: The HN '4-tier self-healing agent silently broken for weeks' post highlights the problem. As agents run longer autonomous tasks, silent failures become more costly. The pattern is well-known (watchdog timers) but not productized for agents.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Low. Heartbeat monitoring and watchdog timers are well-understood patterns. The agent-specific additions (loop detection via output analysis) add moderate complexity. Could ship MVP in 1-2 weeks.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $200-300M (agent monitoring). SAM: $40-60M (watchdog specific). SOM: $4-6M. Best as a feature within a larger reliability toolkit, not standalone.

ranked_gaps.title: Non-Developer Agent Reliability Configuration
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A visual/no-code interface for configuring agent error recovery: drag-and-drop retry policies, guardrail rules, escalation paths, and fallback strategies. Integrates with popular no-code agent platforms (Zapier AI, Make, n8n). Think 'Retool for agent reliability'.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Business users and knowledge workers deploying AI agents via no-code platforms who lack the technical skills to configure error handling. Small businesses using AI assistants that need basic reliability without hiring developers.
ranked_gaps.why_now: No-code AI agent platforms are growing but their users hit a wall when agents fail. The low-code AI market is still maturing, so timing is early but the need is real.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium-High. Building a visual configuration UI is significant work. Integrating with multiple no-code platforms requires custom adapters. The target audience (non-developers) demands a high-quality UX.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $300-500M (no-code AI tooling). SAM: $60-100M (reliability config). SOM: $6-10M. Market is early.

ranked_gaps.title: Error Recovery Analytics Dashboard
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A specialized analytics dashboard for agent error recovery metrics: error frequency by type, recovery success rate, MTTR, cost-per-recovery, recovery strategy effectiveness, and trends over time. Integrates with existing observability tools (LangSmith, Langfuse, Braintrust) as an add-on layer.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Platform engineering teams managing 10+ agents who need to optimize recovery strategies based on data. Teams with SLA requirements on agent uptime/reliability.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Observability tools (LangSmith, Langfuse, AgentOps, Braintrust) are mature and growing but none offer recovery-specific analytics. However, these tools could easily add these features as incremental updates, making this a feature rather than a product.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Dashboard UI and metric aggregation are standard. The differentiation is in recovery-specific metric design and integration with existing observability data.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: TAM: $800M-1.2B (AI observability). SAM: $50-100M (recovery analytics). SOM: $5-10M. Risk: easily built as a feature by existing observability players.
